name: Ketone body metabolism (ketogenesis and ketolysis)
summary: Hepatic synthesis of the ketone bodies acetoacetate, D-β-hydroxybutyrate and acetone from fatty-acid-derived acetyl-CoA, and their oxidation back to acetyl-CoA for TCA-cycle fuel in extrahepatic tissues.
description:
Ketone bodies — acetoacetate, D-β-hydroxybutyrate (D-3-hydroxybutyrate) and acetone — are water-soluble, lipid-derived fuels produced by the liver and combusted by extrahepatic tissues (brain, heart, skeletal muscle, kidney) during fasting, prolonged exercise, ketogenic diets, the neonatal period and diabetic ketoacidosis.

**Ketogenesis** (hepatic mitochondrial matrix) condenses acetyl-CoA generated by fatty-acid β-oxidation: acetoacetyl-CoA thiolase (ACAT1/T2) makes acetoacetyl-CoA, mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase (HMGCS2) — the committed, rate-limiting step — adds a third acetyl unit to form (S)-HMG-CoA, and HMG-CoA lyase (HMGCL) cleaves it to acetoacetate plus acetyl-CoA. Acetoacetate is reduced to D-β-hydroxybutyrate by BDH1 (poised by the mitochondrial NADH/NAD+ ratio), or slowly and non-enzymatically decarboxylates to acetone.

**Ketolysis** (extrahepatic mitochondria) runs the sequence in reverse: BDH1 re-oxidizes β-hydroxybutyrate to acetoacetate, SCOT (OXCT1) — the rate-limiting ketolytic enzyme — transfers CoA from succinyl-CoA to yield acetoacetyl-CoA, and thiolase splits it into two acetyl-CoA that enter the TCA cycle. **The liver cannot oxidize the ketone bodies it makes because hepatocytes do not express SCOT**, which enforces net export to the periphery.

Control is dominated by substrate supply (adipose lipolysis and β-oxidation flux) and by HMGCS2: succinyl-CoA succinylates and inactivates HMGCS2, SIRT3 deacetylates and activates it, and PPARα (with PGC-1α) transcriptionally induces it during fasting; glucagon promotes and insulin suppresses the whole program. Beyond fuel, D-β-hydroxybutyrate is a signaling metabolite — an endogenous class I histone-deacetylase inhibitor and an agonist of the HCAR2/GPR109A receptor.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 17 (Fatty acid catabolism / ketone bodies)

references.title: Multi-dimensional roles of ketone bodies in fuel metabolism, signaling, and therapeutics (Puchalska & Crawford)
Regulation of ketone body metabolism and the role of PPARα (Grabacka et al.)
SIRT3 deacetylates mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase 2 and regulates ketone body production (Shimazu et al.)
Succinylation and inactivation of HMG-CoA synthase by succinyl-CoA and its relevance to the control of ketogenesis (Lowe & Tubbs)
Suppression of oxidative stress by β-hydroxybutyrate, an endogenous histone deacetylase inhibitor (Shimazu et al.)
Successful adaptation to ketosis by mice with tissue-specific deficiency of ketone body oxidation (SCOT) (Cotter et al.)

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: Site of hepatic ketogenesis and extrahepatic ketolysis.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial inner membrane
compartments.description: Membrane that anchors BDH1; its catalytic site faces the matrix.

compartments.name: Nucleus
compartments.description: Compartment where PPARα transactivates the HMGCS2 gene.

metabolites.name: Glucagon
metabolites.description: Fasting/counter-regulatory hormone that drives hepatic ketogenesis via cAMP/PKA.

metabolites.name: Insulin
metabolites.description: Fed-state hormone that suppresses lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis.

enzymes.name: Acetyl-CoA acetyltransferase, mitochondrial (T2 thiolase)
enzymes.description: Potassium-activated acetoacetyl-CoA thiolase; condenses two acetyl-CoA in ketogenesis and cleaves acetoacetyl-CoA in ketolysis.

enzymes.name: Hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA synthase, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Committed, rate-limiting enzyme of ketogenesis; heavily regulated by succinylation, SIRT3 deacetylation and PPARα-driven transcription.

enzymes.name: D-β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Lecithin-requiring, membrane-bound NAD+-dependent dehydrogenase interconverting acetoacetate and D-β-hydroxybutyrate; direction set by the NADH/NAD+ ratio.

enzymes.name: Succinyl-CoA:3-ketoacid CoA transferase 1 (SCOT)
enzymes.description: Rate-limiting enzyme of ketolysis; activates acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA using succinyl-CoA. Not expressed in hepatocytes, so the liver cannot consume ketone bodies.

enzymes.name: NAD-dependent protein deacetylase sirtuin-3, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Fasting-activated mitochondrial deacetylase; deacetylates HMGCS2 (K310/K447/K473) to boost ketogenesis.

enzymes.name: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPARα)
enzymes.description: Ligand-activated (fatty acid / acyl-CoA) transcription factor; master transcriptional regulator of fasting ketogenesis that induces HMGCS2, FGF21 and β-oxidation genes.

reactions.name: Spontaneous decarboxylation of acetoacetate to acetone

sources.id: Lehninger 8e (acetone arises by slow non-enzymatic decarboxylation of acetoacetate)

reactions.name: Acetoacetate activation by SCOT (committed step of ketolysis)

regulations.mechanism: Succinyl-CoA succinylates the active-site cysteine of mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase, inactivating it; the extent of succinylation sets ketogenic flux and is decreased by fasting/glucagon.
SIRT3 deacetylates HMGCS2 at Lys310/Lys447/Lys473, enhancing its enzymatic activity and increasing β-hydroxybutyrate production during fasting.
Fasting- and fatty-acid-activated PPARα (with coactivator PGC-1α) transactivates the HMGCS2 gene, raising hepatic ketogenic capacity.
Glucagon (via cAMP/PKA in fasting) promotes hepatic ketogenesis, in part by decreasing succinylation of HMG-CoA synthase and inducing its expression.
Insulin (fed state) suppresses ketogenesis: it inhibits adipose lipolysis (limiting fatty-acid substrate), raises malonyl-CoA to block CPT1-mediated fatty-acid entry, and represses HMGCS2 expression.

relations.note: HMGCS2 gene encodes the rate-limiting ketogenic enzyme.
Mitochondrial β-oxidation of fatty acids supplies the acetyl-CoA that fuels hepatic ketogenesis.
Acetoacetyl-CoA and HMG-CoA are shared with the cytosolic mevalonate/cholesterol pathway (via HMGCS1 and HMG-CoA reductase).
In extrahepatic tissues, ketolysis-derived acetyl-CoA enters the TCA cycle for terminal oxidation.
SCOT draws succinyl-CoA from the TCA cycle to activate acetoacetate, coupling ketolysis to the succinyl-CoA/succinate node (bypassing succinyl-CoA synthetase).
Ketogenic amino acids (leucine, lysine, phenylalanine, tyrosine) feed HMG-CoA/acetoacetate, adding to the ketone body pool.
Beyond fuel, D-β-hydroxybutyrate is a signaling metabolite: an endogenous class I HDAC inhibitor and an agonist of the HCAR2/GPR109A receptor.
